claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 13 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start with the basics:

**How many children would you like to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! I'm looking to claim for my two children. Alex is 13 and Blake is 15.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great to know you're looking to claim for Alex (13) and Blake (15).

Now, could you tell me: **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you for confirming that.

**Do both Alex and Blake live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with me, but Blake doesn't live with me at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

Since Alex lives with you, that's straightforward. For Blake, I need to ask a few more questions.

**Regarding Blake, who doesn't live with you:**

Do you contribute towards Blake's upkeep (for example, by paying for food, clothes, pocket money, or other expenses)? If so, is the amount you contribute at least equal to the Child Benefit rate (currently £25.60 per week for an eldest/only child, or £16.95 per week for additional children)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Blake's upkeep. I help with clothes, school things, and other expenses. It comes to about £26 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know.

A couple more questions about Blake:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake?** For example, is the person Blake is living with claiming for them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

Just a couple more questions to complete my assessment:

**Are either Alex or Blake in any of the following situations?**
- In hospital or residential care
- In local authority care
- Working 24 or more hours a week
- Receiving benefits in their own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake has been in local authority care for a little while now, but it's only been a couple of months. Alex isn't in any of those situations.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information.

I have a couple more questions about Blake's care situation:

1. **Is the local council paying anything towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance while they're in care?**

2. **Does Blake spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you, or spend 2 nights in a row each week or 7 or more days in a row at home?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance. And yes, Blake does come home to stay with me regularly - they spend time at home each week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying those details. That's really helpful.

Just to confirm regarding Blake's time at home: **Does Blake spend at least 24 hours a week at your home, or at least 2 consecutive nights each week, or 7 or more days in a row at home?**

I want to make sure I understand the arrangement correctly to give you accurate information.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake spends at least 24 hours a week at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming that. I now have all the information I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm pleased to tell you that **you appear to be eligible for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake**.

Here's a summary:

### Alex (age 13) ✅ Eligible
- Alex is under 16 years old
- You live in the UK
- Alex lives with you
- Alex is not in any situation that would affect eligibility (such as hospital, care, working 24+ hours, or receiving benefits)

### Blake (age 15) ✅ Eligible
Although Blake is in local authority care, you still qualify because:
- Blake is under 16 years old
- The local council is **not** paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance
- You contribute towards Blake's upkeep (approximately £26 per week, which meets the required threshold)
- Blake spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you, which is an important exception that allows Child Benefit to continue
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake

### Next Steps
If you haven't already, you can make a claim for Child Benefit. Please note that if you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may be subject to the High Income Child Benefit Tax Charge.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 13 years old (under 16), you live in the UK, and Alex lives with you. This meets the basic eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. Alex is not in hospital, care, or receiving benefits in their own right, and is not working 24+ hours a week. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 15 years old (under 16) and you live in the UK. Although Blake is in local authority care and doesn't live with you full-time, several factors support your eligibility: (1) The local council is not paying anything towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance, (2) You contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit rate (approximately £26 per week) towards Blake's upkeep, (3) Blake spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you, which is an exception that allows Child Benefit to continue for children in care, and (4) No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for both of your children. You live in the UK and meet the responsibility criteria for both children. Alex lives with you and meets standard eligibility. Blake, although in local authority care, qualifies under the exceptions because the council isn't paying for their accommodation/maintenance, you contribute towards their upkeep, and Blake spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you.

Alex is not in hospital, care, or receiving benefits in their own right, and is not working 24+ hours a week. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 15 years old (under 16) and you live in the UK. Although Blake is in local authority care and doesn't live with you full-time, several factors support your eligibility: (1) The local council is not paying anything towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance, (2) You contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit rate (approximately £26 per week) towards Blake's upkeep, (3) Blake spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you, which is an exception that allows Child Benefit to continue for children in care, and (4) No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for both of your children. You live in the UK and meet the responsibility criteria for both children. Alex lives with you and meets standard eligibility. Blake, although in local authority care, qualifies under the exceptions because the council isn't paying for their accommodation/maintenance, you contribute towards their upkeep, and Blake spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you.
